message.abstract:
<jats:title>Abstract</jats:title>
                <jats:p>
                  Non-pharmaceutical interventions (NPIs) have been a cornerstone in managing emergent diseases such as COVID-19
                  <jats:sup>1–4</jats:sup>
                  . However, despite their potential to contain or attenuate the epidemic, the effects of NPIs on disease dynamics are not well understood
                  <jats:sup>1,5–7</jats:sup>
                  . We show that saturation of NPIs with the increase in infected individuals, an expected consequence of limited contact tracing and healthcare capacities, produces a positive feedback in the disease growth rate and a threshold between two alternative states--containment and outbreak
                  <jats:sup>8</jats:sup>
                  . These alternative states were previously related with the strength of NPIs but not with the infection number
                  <jats:sup>2,9–11</jats:sup>
                  . Furthermore, the transition between these states involves an abrupt acceleration in disease dynamics, which we report here for several COVID-19 outbreaks around the world. The consequences of a positive feedback in population dynamics at low numbers is a phenomenon widely studied in ecology--the Allee effect. This effect is a determinant of extinction-outbreak states, geographic synchronization, spatial spread, and the effect of exogenous variables, as vaccination
                  <jats:sup>12–15</jats:sup>
                  . As countries are relaxing containing measures, recognizing an NPI-induced Allee effect may be essential for deploying containment strategies within and among countries
                  <jats:sup>16</jats:sup>
                  and acknowledges the need for early warning indicators of approaching epidemic tipping points
                  <jats:sup>17</jats:sup>
                  .
                </jats:p>
title: Containment to outbreak tipping points in COVID-19

Ferretti, L. et al. Quantifying SARS-CoV-2 transmission suggests epidemic control with digital contact tracing. Science 368 (2020).
Hellewell, J. et al. Feasibility of controlling COVID-19 outbreaks by isolation of cases and contacts. The Lancet Global Health (2020).
Siegenfeld, A. F. , Taleb, N. N. & Bar-Yam, Y. Opinion: What models can and cannot tell us about COVID-19. Proc. Natl Acad. Sci. USA (2020).
Berryman, A. A. Principles of Population Dynamics and Their Application. (Stanley Thornes, 1999).
Ruktanonchai, N. W. et al. Assessing the impact of coordinated COVID-19 exit strategies across Europe. Science (2020).
